utterances: This is another whole contentious area of the bill, and maybe we shouldn't go there.
But, all I would say is that the most important thing is that they're going to be legal.
And that's going to help them bargain for the wage…
Are you still worried about it, Caroline?
I think she's left us.
Anyway, thanks very much, Caroline, for the call.
And let's go now to Giles(ph) and Giles is with us from Minneapolis.
Yes, good day.
Good day.
Well, my point of view - we're looking it from a very U.S.-centric point of view and I'd reverse the equation.
And look at what you could be doing to other countries.
Other countries spent a lot of money training, let's say, nurses, doctors, engineers.
And they're not going to be paid a fraction of what they can get in the United States.
We've already seen this in Europe.
We are attracting skilled people from other countries where they're more needed than here.
And I think we should really be looking at how we educate our own workforce and fill the gaps that are needed.
That's really kind of two questions of - I want to go to Jeanne Butterfield.
And certainly, education and training are an important piece of the equation, and we could have a whole another session on the adequacy of our education system.
But I know we're not going to go there right now as well.
But I also would say that, you know, in terms of labor force demographics, we are an aging workforce in this country.
We are not replacing ourselves at the rate we need.
We're also an expanding economy, and baby boomers like myself are also hoping to retire sometime soon.
And when you look at both demographics compiled together, you are looking at labor force needs into the next decade and beyond and beyond that are really quite astounding.
The president's own economic adviser talks about the need for 1.8 million new workers in this country simply to keep up with the expanding economy and…
And another issue too.
But…
The caller makes a fair point about the other countries, but…
The brain drain.
The brain drain part.
Brain drain, yes.
We need both.
Tamar Jacoby.
But I'll never forget, excuse me, the man I met who invented the Pentium chip.
And he was from India, an engineer from India who'd come here as a graduate student and made his way up at Intel and eventually invented the Pentium chip.
And at home in India, he was quite clear and absolutely had no doubt.
He would never have invented the Pentium chip because they didn't have the research infrastructure and the companies it needed…
At that time.
They might now.
Well, they might now.

But the point was that he gave a contribution to the world that he couldn't have given if he had stayed home in India that he was able to give because he came here where - for everybody - where the action was at that time in that science.
And so, I think, the brain drain is a point, but it's not the only way to think about this.
And Giles, as another way to think about it, we're in competition with those other countries, shouldn't we try to attract the best and the brightest?
Yeah.
We are in competition with them.
But we, you know, are we in competition with the Caribbean countries or wherever to strip their nurses and doctors, which we do - well, the Western world does.
And I'm not sure the shape of the economy 20 years now.
I'm a baby boomer too.
And I don't think the shape the economy is not really going to be the same in 20 years as people think.
I don't think you're extrapolating straight lines, and I think there are adjustments to be made.
And I think a lot of baby boomers are going to be finding that they're going to be working off at 65.
Well…
We're going to need…
We're going to need the workers.
We're going to need nursing care and nursing home care.
And we're going to need childcare going forward, you know.
Let's train ourselves.
(Unintelligible) from other people who need it too.
Well.
Hmm.
Giles, thanks very much for the call.
Thank you.
But you raised some interesting questions.
Here's an email from Paul in Rochester, New York.
In deciding who's welcome in the United States, first, let's choose those really want to become Americans, contribute to our society and stay here for the rest of their lives.
Move to second place, those whose sole motivation is strictly economic.
We need to look at the long term.
I'm not sure that I see a distinction there.
Yeah.
That's a nice point.
I mean, I would say that many, if not most, immigrants come to make money, but stay because they fall in love with it.
And, I think, that's exactly what we want.
And, I think, he's got a good point.
Let's have both the people who love it and people who were…
I think that also points very much to the distinctions between our temporary immigration visas…
Mm-hmm.
…and system and our permanent…
Green cards.
…status that leads - green cards - that lead to citizenship.
And we need both because there certainly are a portion of people in the world who want to come.
They want to make their mark.
They want to earn money.
They want to go back home, build their home, start their small business.
And we have a need for them on our economy.
And they want that circularity.
Our current system doesn't allow that because we don't provide a temporary visa for people in the essential services in our economy to come in at all.
And that's why we have such a huge undocumented flow.
So I would argue while we're focusing on the long-term permanent status -people today - we do need both.
Well, of course, we need both.
And the bill would provide…
Tamar Jacoby, go ahead.
the bill would provide for both.
Right, right.
I mean, I think in the ideal world, we would actually have everyone come on a temporary visa, skilled and unskilled.
And your temporary stint would be your tryout, and then we would apply the merit-point system at the end of the tryout.
And it would say not just what skill do you have, but how well did you do here.
And did you have the grit and whatever it takes in America, and did you come to love it.
But it sounds as if neither of you were in the negotiations between the Senate and the White House.
No.
Fortunately, we didn't control the pen.
(Soundbite of laughter)
Let's see if we can get another caller on the line.
This is Virginia, Virginia with us from Aiken in South Carolina.
Yes, this is Virginia.
I would say that we should look to the lower educated families to come because we need their assistance here, and so the farmers and the other growers, their fruits and so forth.
Such a high percentage of our food now is coming from offshore and the people here are having trouble.
And also, those who have the higher education, there's been a big drain in the countries that they're coming from.
No one is staying there once they reach a certain percentage.
So they do not have good doctors.
They don't have good teachers.
They all come to the United States.
I don't blame them because we pay them more.
But I think we need to be a little selfish and let those who can help us come and it will help them also.
And then, of course, you're, certainly, going to allow some of those who have very special skills to come in, but not just everybody because he has a college degree.
